segments.s416-g153: به دیدن ... آمد is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'came to visit' (به + infinitive + آمدن). فزون is placed before the verb آمد and functions adverbially as 'more/more numerous' modifying آمد. از آگهی gives the reason: از here means 'because of/on account of' and آگهی = 'news/information'. The whole clause thus states that an increased number came to visit because of the report.
segments.s416-g154: همی before a past-form verb (تافت) is a classical/poetic device indicating continuous or habitual aspect ('continually/ever did'). تافت is the poetic past of 'to shine'. زو is the contracted sequence ز + او (ز 'from' + او 'him'), an archaic/poetic form meaning 'from him'. فر شاهنشهی is a noun phrase where فر ('divine/royal glory') is qualified by شاهنشهی ('kingly/imperial'), so the clause says the kingly glory shone from him.
segments.s416-g33: This is an optative clause introduced by باد (optative 'may'). The word order is subject (همه دشمنان, 'all enemies') + از + agent/cause (تو, 'because of you') + predicate (پر بیم, 'full of fear') + باد (optative particle). از here marks the cause/reason ('because of/for'), and پر is an adjectival prepositional complement meaning 'full (of)'.
segments.s416-g34: Another optative clause with باد at the end. دل بدسگالان is a possessive/genitive construction 'the hearts of the bad‑fated (ill‑omened) ones' and functions as the subject; به دو نیم باد is an idiomatic result phrase 'may be split into two', where به marks direction/into and نیم is 'half'. The structure is Subject + 'to/into' + complement + optative 'may'.
segments.s416-g35: همه‌ساله 'every year' is an adverbial modifier placed before the subject بخت تو ('your fortune'). پیروز is a predicate adjective 'victorious', and باد is again the optative particle 'may'. Word order: time adverbial + subject + predicate + optative.
segments.s416-g36: This clause uses a verbless optative copular structure: شبان سیه ('dark/black nights') as subject, بر تو ('upon you') marking the beneficiary/target, نوروز ('Nowruz' = renewal/day) as the predicate, and باد as the optative 'may'. The sense is 'may the dark nights become/serve as Nowruz for you.'
segments.s416-g37: چو بشنید is a temporal subordinate clause ('when he heard'); چو = 'when' and بشنید is past simple 'he heard'. گفتارش is the object/genitive 'his speech/utterance' (here likely 'his words'), placed after the verb in poetic word order. اسفندیار is the subject/agent of the main clause reference.
segments.s416-g38: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'came/alighted down' (فرود = descent/alighting, آمد = came). از marks source 'from', and باره نامدار is a genitive phrase 'the famed/renowned occasion/spot' (باره here 'occasion/instance/spot'). The whole means he descended from that renowned place/occasion.
segments.s416-g39: گو here is a narrative particle (literally 'say'/'so said') introducing the action. پیلتن را uses را as the definite direct‑object marker; پیلتن is the object (a place or person). به بر در گرفت is a classical idiom meaning 'took into his arms'—به + بر + در (here 'in') + گرفت (past 'took/seized'). The verb گرفت is simple past 'took'.
segments.s416-g40: چو خشنود شد is a temporal 'when he became pleased' (چو = when; شد = became). آفرین برگرفت is a light idiom: آفرین functions as an exclamation/noun 'a cry of praise' and برگرفت (literally 'took up') means 'he raised/uttered' — together 'he raised a cry of praise.' Word order is temporal clause + then main action.
segments.s416-g105: نیایی is a negative optative/imperative form (literally 'do not come / may you not come'), addressed to تو (2nd person). زمانی here means 'at any time' or 'ever' and modifies the verb phrase; در خان من is a locative prepositional phrase ('in my house / in my hall') with در (in) before the noun (typical Persian order: preposition + noun).
segments.s416-g106: نباشی is the negative 2nd-person subjunctive/imperative 'do not be / be not'. The phrase بدین مرز (به + این مرز) functions as a locative/goal 'in/at this frontier'; مرز is the noun 'frontier/border'. مهمان من ('my guest') is the predicative/nominal complement: 'do not be my guest.' Word order places the negated verb before the locative and nominal complements.
segments.s416-g107: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. این تیزی is the direct object 'this sharpness/point' (تیزی = sharp point/keenness). از مغز بیرون is a prepositional phrase 'out of the brain' (از = out of; مغز = brain; بیرون = out), and کنی is the 2nd-person subjunctive/potential 'you do / you can do' that completes the protasis ('if you do X').
segments.s416-g108: بکوشی is an exhortative/subjunctive 2nd-person verb 'strive / try'. و simply links the two verbs. بر خسته is a preposition + adjective/noun phrase 'upon/on the weary one' (بر = upon; خسته = the tired/weak person). افسون کنی is another verb phrase where افسون (a charm/incantation) is the object and کنی is again the 2nd-person subjunctive 'you do' — together 'cast a charm on / enchant / by incantation heal.'
segments.s416-g109: ز من 'from me / as for me' marks the source. هرچ خواهیت is a free relative 'whatever you wish' (هرچ = whatever; خواهیت = 2nd-person verb 'you want/wish' in an optative/future-subjunctive sense). فرمان کنم is the main clause 'I will do / I will command/grant' with کنم as 1st-person subjunctive/future — together meaning 'I will do whatever you ask.'
segments.s416-g110: ز دیدار تو 'from the sight of you / by seeing you' (ز = from/because of; دیدار = sight/meeting; تو = you). رامش جان کنم is a transitive/cause construction where رامش (tranquility/solace) is the thing brought about and جان (soul) is the possessor — 'I make tranquility of my soul' i.e. 'you bring peace to my soul.' کنَم is first-person present-subjunctive used here as a simple future/performative.
segments.s416-g111: مگر introduces an exception/background 'except/excepting'. بند is the noun 'link/fetter'; کز ('که از' fused) introduces a relative clause 'which/from which'. بند عاری بود = 'was free from the bond/chain' (عاری بودن = to be free from). The clause as a whole = 'except the link which was free from the chain.'
segments.s416-g112: Two short predicative clauses in succession: شکستی بود ('there was a breaking/defeat' — شکستی here expresses a breaking/ruin or defeat) and زشت‌کاری بود ('there was a shameful act'). Each predicate is linked to an existential 'بود' (past/copular), giving a terse summary judgement: 'a rupture occurred; it was disgraceful.'
segments.s416-g41: Here 'که' introduces an exclamatory/subordinate clause roughly meaning 'that' or 'may it be that'; 'یزدان سپاس' is an idiomatic exclamation 'praise/thanks to God' (سپاس as a short interjection). 'ای جهان‌پهلوان' is a vocative—'O world‑champion'—addressing the addressee directly.
segments.s416-g42: 'که' links this clause to the previous exclamation ('for/that'); 'دیدم' is the 1st‑person past 'I saw' (classical perfective). 'ترا' is the object (ت + را: 2nd‑person + object marker) meaning 'you'. 'شاد و روشن‌روان' are two predicate adjectives: 'happy' and 'bright‑souled/cheerful', coordinated by و.
segments.s416-g43: 'سزاوار باشد' is an impersonal construction meaning 'it is fitting/appropriate' (باشد here is 3rd‑person subjunctive/optative giving a sense 'may it be'). 'ستودن' is the infinitive 'to praise', and 'ترا' (ت + را) is its object 'you' placed after the verbal noun as is common in Persian.
segments.s416-g44: This is a compact verbless clause: 'یلان جهان' = 'the heroes/champions of the world' (noun phrase), 'خاک بودن' = 'to be dust' (خاک + infinitive بودن), and 'ترا' (t + ra) marks the person to whom that dust‑status pertains—'to be your dust' i.e. humbly beneath you. Word order is noun phrase + predicate infinitive + object suffix.
segments.s416-g45: 'خنک' used at the start is an exclamatory/ evaluative adjective 'pleasant/fortunate'. 'آنک' is a relative/introductory particle 'that/for which', and the phrase 'چون تو پسر باشدش' literally 'a son like you be‑to‑him' uses 'چون' = 'like', 'پسر' = 'son', and 'باشدش' = باشد + enclitic ش (here ش = 'for him' or 'to him') giving the sense 'that he has a son like you.'
segments.s416-g46: 'یکی' = 'one/a single'; 'شاخ' here is a concrete image 'branch/bough' (poetic), and 'بیند' is 3rd‑person present/past narrative 'he sees' (classical poetic present can be used narratively). 'که' introduces a relative clause 'that', and 'بر باشدش' (بر = 'on/over', باشدش = 'is for him') describes the branch as 'being over/on him' or 'set above him'—a figurative support or emblem.
segments.s416-g47: 'خنک آنک' again opens with 'how pleasant/fortunate that'; 'او را بود' literally 'it was for him' (او = 'he', را = object/dative marker, بود = past 'was') functioning idiomatically as 'he had'. 'چون تو پشت' = 'a back/support like you' where 'چون' = 'like' precedes the noun; together: 'How fortunate that he had you for a back/support.'
segments.s416-g48: 'بود' is the past copula 'was'; 'ایمن' is an adjective 'safe/protected'; 'از' is the preposition 'from'; 'روزگار' here carries the sense 'fate / the course of days', and 'درشت' is an attributive adjective 'harsh/rough/cruel' modifying روزگار. Together: 'he was safe from the harshness of fate.'
segments.s416-g113: نبیند is a negative jussive/subjunctive (3rd person) meaning 'may he/she/one not see' or 'let no one see'. مرا (here split as م + را) is the clitic object 'me' with را as the definite/object marker placed after the clitic; classical Persian allows the object after the verb. با بند کس is a prepositional phrase 'in/with fetters' plus کس ('anyone/no one'); in this negative optative context کس functions effectively as 'no one'.
segments.s416-g114: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for/because/that'). روشن روانم is a noun + possessive: روشن 'bright/radiant' modifying روان 'soul/spirit', with the enclitic -م 'my'. برینست is an archaic/poetic fused form (literally orthography corresponds to برین است); here it functions predicatively ('is exalted/above/beyond' — the precise lexeme is archaic, so the entry in the given senses doesn't match directly). و بس is a final particle meaning 'and that is enough' or 'and nothing more.'
segments.s416-g115: ز (poetic) + تو + پیش gives a temporal/relative phrase 'before you' or 'previous to you' (ز ... پیش = 'before'). بودند is the past third-person plural of بودن 'to be' ('there were' / 'they were present'). کنداوران is a plural agent noun ('kandavaran') — literally 'those who dig/excavate' or figuratively 'those who came before/previous challengers' — used here to name earlier people.
segments.s416-g116: نکردند is the past negative of کردن (3rd pl.) 'they did not do / they did not cause'. پایم is 'my foot' (پا + enclitic -م). به here marks direction/into ('into/onto the fetters'). بند گران is a noun phrase 'heavy/weighty fetters' (بند = fetter, گران = heavy); the object phrase follows the verb complex, consistent with Persian order.
segments.s416-g117: به پاسخ چنین گفتش is a conventional formula: به پاسخ 'in reply' (به + noun), چنین 'thus/so', گفتش = گفت + -ش (he said to him/said it to him). اسفندیار is the speaker's name in apposition; the whole segment means 'Esfandiyar said thus in reply (to him)'.
segments.s416-g118: که‌ای is an archaic/vocative construction (که + ای) used to address someone 'O you' or introduce a quotative clause. در جهان 'in the world' sets the domain. از گوان یادگار literally 'a remembrance/of the like' — از + گوان ('of/among the like') + یادگار ('memorial, reminder, exemplar'), i.e. 'a memorial/exemplar among his kind in the world.' The clause is an emphatic epithet addressed to the addressee.
segments.s416-g119: همه راست گفتی نگفتی دروغ is compact: همه راست گفتی 'you spoke all truth / you told nothing but the truth' (همه = wholly/all, راست = true, گفتی = you said). نگفتی دروغ literally 'you did not speak falsehood' — the pair reinforces honesty. Note classical Persian often stacks positive and negative formulations for emphasis.
segments.s416-g120: به here introduces the object or manner ('to/from' — in this idiom: 'to crookedness'). نگیرند is present-subjunctive/negative 3rd pl. of گرفتن used here intransitively 'do not take up / do not resort to'. مردان فروغ is the subject: مردان 'men' (in epic often 'heroes') + فروغ 'splendor/glory' (an epithet). Together: 'men of splendor do not resort to crookedness.'
segments.s416-g9: 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before the temporal particle 'تا' which here means 'when/once'; 'رسیدند' is past tense, 3rd person plural 'they arrived/reached'; 'نزدیک آب' is a locative phrase 'near the water' (adverbial complement). Word order is adverb + temporal particle + verb + locative.
segments.s416-g10: 'به دیدار' is a directional phrase 'toward the meeting / to see (one another)'; 'هر دو' means 'both' (each of the two); 'گرفته شتاب' is a compound in which the participle 'گرفته' ('taken/seized') modifies the noun 'شتاب' ('haste'), yielding 'took haste / became eager' → 'hastened'. The whole phrase says they both moved quickly toward meeting.
segments.s416-g11: 'تهمتن' is the subject (proper name/epithet). 'ز' is the archaic/prepositional 'from'. Here 'ز خشک' is a poetic expression meaning 'off the (steed/saddle)' — 'خشک' in this line functions as a metonym for the mount (poetic usage), so 'ز خشک اندر آمد' = 'came down from the horse / alighted'. 'اندر آمد' is a compound verb 'came in/approached', and 'به رود' is the directional complement 'to the river'.
segments.s416-g12: 'پیاده شد' is a light verb construction meaning 'he became on foot' → 'he dismounted / went on foot'. 'و' links clauses. In the second clause 'داد' is the past verb 'gave' and the object follows with the object marker 'را': 'یل را درود' = 'a greeting to the hero' (i.e. 'he greeted the hero' or 'he gave the hero a salute').
segments.s416-g13: 'پس از آفرین گفت' is a sequence: 'پس' = then/after, 'از آفرین گفتن' is the idiom 'to utter praise / to exclaim “bravo”', and 'گفت' is the reporting verb 'said'. 'کز' (contracted 'که از') introduces the content clause 'that/from'. 'یک خدای' literally 'one God' (i.e. 'God'); word order is somewhat elliptical/poetic.
segments.s416-g14: 'همی خواستم' is an imperfective/past habitual: 'I have long wished / I was wishing'; 'تا' introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause 'that/so that'; 'بود' is the copula in a jussive/subjunctive sense 'might be / would be'; 'رهنمای' is the noun 'guide'. Together: 'I have always wished that there be a guide.'
segments.s416-g15: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause 'that / who'. 'با نامداران' means 'with the renowned (men) / accompanied by famed warriors'. 'بدین' (here written as 'ب' + 'دین' in the wordlist) is the demonstrative/directional 'to/at this' (poetic 'به این'), and 'جایگاه' is 'place/position' — 'to this place'. The phrase locates arrival and companionship.
segments.s416-g16: 'چنین' 'thus/so' modifies the following predicate; 'تندرست آید' is 'may come/arrive healthy' — 'آید' is present/jussive 'come' and 'تندرست' is predicative 'healthy'. 'و با سپاه' adds 'and with an army' ('با' = with, 'سپاه' = army/host). The clause expresses the hoped-for manner of his arrival.
segments.s416-g73: Literal order: the object ('بند') and location ('بر پای') come before the verb. 'تو خود' is emphatic 'you yourself'. The word 'نه' here is an archaic imperative of نهادن ('put, place'), so the clause means 'You yourself put the fetter on your foot.' 'بی‌درنگ' is an adverb 'immediately/at once' modifying the imperative.
segments.s416-g74: The verb 'نباشد' is an impersonal/optative form of بودن used here to mean 'there will be no / it is not'; it heads the clause. 'ز' = 'from/of', and 'بند شهنشاه' means 'the fetter of the emperor' (possessive/genitive). 'ننگ' is the predicate noun 'shame/disgrace' — literally 'there will be no shame from the emperor's fetter.'
segments.s416-g75: 'ترا' is written here as two pieces: the 2nd-person object suffix 'ت' plus the object marker 'را' (both mark 'you' as direct object). 'چون' functions as 'when/as' in a temporal clause. 'برم' is a verbal form of بردن meaning 'bring/take' (here 'they bring/lead'), and 'بسته' is a participle 'bound/tied' modifying the object. 'نزدیک شاه' is the locative 'near/before the king.'
segments.s416-g76: 'سراسر' intensifies totality ('entirely/altogether'). 'بدو' = 'to him' (dative), and 'بازگردد' is the verb 'returns/falls back' (impersonal/3rd-person). 'گناه' here means 'fault/blame' — the clause says that the whole blame will be returned to him.
segments.s416-g77: 'وزین' is an adjective 'weighty/heavy' modifying 'بستگی' ('attachment/obligation'). 'من' marks possession 'my.' The phrase 'جگر خسته‌ام' places the noun 'جگر' ('heart, seat of feeling') with the predicate adjective 'خسته‌ام' ('I am tired'), so 'my heart is weary.'
segments.s416-g78: 'به پیش تو' = 'in/ before you' (prepositional phrase: 'in your presence'). 'اندر' is a locative preposition 'in/within.' 'کمر بسته‌ام' is a perfect/ resultative construction: 'I have bound/girt my waist' (بسته + -ام = 'I have bound'), i.e. 'I have girded myself.' The verb is first-person singular.
segments.s416-g79: 'نمانم' is first-person negative of ماندن ('I will not remain / I will not stay'). The conjunction 'که' introduces the subordinate clause; here the clause 'تا شب بمانی به بند' means 'that you remain in bonds until night' ('تا' = 'until', 'بمانی' = 'you remain', 'به بند' = 'in fetters'). The whole phrase expresses refusal/negation of staying so that the addressee would be kept bound until night.
segments.s416-g80: 'وگر' = 'but if / if however'. 'بر تو آید' is an impersonal/periphrastic phrasing meaning 'befall/come upon you' (آید = 'comes/happens', with 'بر' = 'upon'). 'ز چیزی' = 'from anything' and 'گزند' = 'harm/damage' — together 'if any harm should befall you from anything.' This is a conditional clause introduced by وگر.
segments.s416-g25: نمانی همی + چز ... را is an archaic/clasic Persian existential construction: نمانی (from ماندن) with the continuous marker همی yields an emphatic present sense 'will remain/ be left'; چز 'except' introduces the sole survivor; سیاوخش را is the excepted item with را marking it as definite/emphatic. Word order (verb → exception phrase) is typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s416-g26: مر is an archaic dative/benefactive form (roughly 'to me' / 'for me'), placed before the noun phrase for emphasis; آن is the demonstrative 'that'; تاج‌دار is a compound epithet 'crown-bearer'; جهان‌بخش is another honorific 'world-bestower/ world-giver'; را again marks the NP as definite/object of what 'remains'. Together the phrase specifies the one thing left to the speaker.
segments.s416-g27: خنک here is a poetic adjective meaning 'fortunate/blessed' (literally 'cool' but used idiomatically); شاه is the head noun 'king'; کو is the classical relative/particle 'who' placed after the noun; چون تو 'such as you' modifies پسر; دارد + پسر is the verb 'has' followed by its object 'son' (word order verb → object is normal in older Persian poetic lines).
segments.s416-g28: به بالا is a prepositional phrase 'to/on high' or 'upwards'; و links that phrase with فرت, an archaic/obscure noun here meaning a kind of exaltation or sudden exultant sound; بنازد is a classical 3rd-person-present verb (from نازیدن) 'takes pride/boasts' and its subject پدر follows it (inversion for poetic emphasis): 'the father boasts (to the heights and with exultation)'.
segments.s416-g29: خنک (again 'fortunate/blessed') modifies شهر ایران 'the city of Iran'; که introduces a relative clause 'that/who'; تخت + ت is 'throne' with the 2nd-person enclitic ت 'your' attached (in the word breakdown the possessive appears separately); را is the definite/object marker tied to the coming verb. The whole segment sets up 'the city of Iran that (people) worship your throne...'.
segments.s416-g30: پرستند is the classical 3rd-person-plural present 'they worship' (used as a general present-tense statement); بیدار is an adjective 'awake/alert' qualifying بخت 'fate/fortune'; ت is again the 2nd-person suffix 'your'; را finishes the object phrase. The sequence effectively prays/notes that the city worships your throne and that your fortune is (or may be) wakeful/alert.
segments.s416-g31: دژم بخت is a classical compound 'grim/ominous fate' (دژم modifying بخت); آنکس 'that person' (or 'whoever') is the indefinite subject introduced by که 'who/that'; با تو نبرد is 'fights you' (با تو 'with you'; نبرد is a martial noun used as or equivalent to 'engage in battle' in epic diction). The clause means 'grim is the fate of anyone who battles you.'
segments.s416-g32: بجوید is a classical 3rd-person-present 'seeks/attempts' (prefix بـ is the old continuous/formative); ز تخت 'from the throne' is the source phrase; اندر آید is 'enters/comes in' (اندر = 'into/within', آید = 'comes'); به گرد 'to/into the circle/round' — together they describe the would-be challenger who seeks the prize and will come down from his throne into the arena/contest. Word order and auxiliary prefixes are typical of Ferdowsi's archaic style.
segments.s416-g89: This clause begins with the preposition ز (from) + pronoun من (me), plus the adverb نیز (also) to mark inclusiveness. یابی is the 2nd-person present form 'you find' / 'you will find' (classical narrative uses present to mean a general or future occurrence). بسی خواسته is a noun phrase 'many/enough wishes' (بسی here quantifies خواسته).
segments.s416-g90: که introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that'); گردد is a subjunctive/optative/present-form of 'become' meaning 'may become' or 'become'; بر و بومت is a compound-like phrase ('bar and bōm' = your land/ground and home) where بر here functions as the first member and بومت is بوم + -ت 'your homeland'; آراسته is a participial/adjectival predicate 'adorned'. The verb گرد(د) governs the predicate 'آراسته'.
segments.s416-g91: بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him' (دو- motion/indirect object before the verb is common in classical Persian). گفت is past tense 'said' (word-order: indirect object + verb + subject). رستم is the subject/name. که‌ای is a poetic vocative ('که' + 'ای' contracted here) addressing someone: 'O ...'; نامدار is the vocative noun 'renowned/famed'.
segments.s416-g92: همی is a classical verbal particle that gives a continuous or habitual sense ('long/always' or 'still'); جستم is the 1st-person past of جستن here 'I sought' (so the clause reads 'I have long sought / I sought repeatedly'). از marks the source 'from'; داور کردگار is a two-word title 'the Judge, the Creator' (داور used here in the sense of the divine judge).
segments.s416-g93: که again introduces purpose ('so that / in order that'). خرم is an adjective 'glad, joyful' modifying دل (heart). کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I may make / I make' with the subject implied (the speaker). به + دیدار تو is an instrumental phrase 'by/through the sight/meeting of you' ('seeing you'). Word order places the object دل before the verb کنم for emphasis.
segments.s416-g94: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. چون here is an interrogative 'how' (introducing a rhetorical question). پسندم is 1st-person present 'I would like / I approve / I desire' (here rhetorical: 'how could I desire...'). من is the emphatic pronoun 'I', placed after the verb for stress. آزار تو 'your harm' is the object (literally 'the harming of you'), so the clause reads 'how could I desire your harm?'
segments.s416-g95: دو + گردن here denotes 'two persons' (literally 'two necks') with گردن فراز 'high-necked / proud' describing them; فرازیم carries the 1st-person plural ending -یم ('we are [high-raised/proud]'). پیر و جوان follows as apposition 'old and young', enumerating the two individuals.
segments.s416-g96: خردمند و بیدار are two adjectival qualities 'wise and alert' that modify the final noun. دو پهلوان 'two champions' is the head noun phrase; note poetic adjective-before-noun order and the repetition of 'دو' to emphasize there are two heroes described by the preceding qualities.
segments.s416-g121: ولیکن is a conjunctive adversative ('but/yet') introducing a contrast. پشوتن is the personal name and is the grammatical subject placed before the verb. شناسد is a classical third‑person singular verb form from شناختن meaning 'knows/recognizes'; که begins the subordinate clause whose content follows (شاه will be the subject of that clause).
segments.s416-g122: چه فرمود is a nominal question/indirect interrogation structure meaning 'what he said/commanded'; فرمود is a past/polite verb here carrying the force of a command or utterance. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that/in order that'); من برفتم is the speaker's action ('I set out' — here a classical perfect/past or optative sense), and به راه is a prepositional phrase 'onto/toward the road.'
segments.s416-g123: گر is the conditional conjunction 'if'. اکنون is an adverb 'now'. بیایم is first‑person present/subjunctive ('I come' / 'I should come') used here in the protasis of the conditional. سوی is the preposition 'toward/to', and خان تو is a possessive construction where the noun (خان, here 'khan'/'court' or 'house') is followed by the possessive pronoun تو ('your'), a common order in classical Persian.
segments.s416-g124: بوم is 'dwelling/abode' (here indicating being at home or in one's place). شاد و پیروز are adjectival predicates 'happy and victorious' describing the speaker's state. مهمان تو ('your guest') places مهمان (guest) before the possessive تو; the whole phrase lists the states the speaker would have on arriving.
segments.s416-g125: تو is the second‑person pronoun 'you'. گردن بپیچی is an idiom meaning 'you bend/submit your neck' (بپیچی is the second‑person present/subjunctive/optative form of پیچیدن meaning 'to bend/turn'); ز here means 'because of/from' and introduces the cause, followed by فرمان شاه 'the king's command' (فرمان = command, شاه = king).
segments.s416-g126: This line contains a topical/object pronoun plus the predicate: مرا (here split in the segmentation as م + را) marks the experiencer/object 'for me'; تابش روز ('the shining of the day') is the subject phrase; گردد is the verb 'becomes/turns'; سیاه is the predicate adjective 'black/dark'. Poetic inversion places the object/experiencer before the subject and verb.
segments.s416-g127: دگر here means 'again/anew'. آنک‌گر is an archaic connective/relative used to introduce the conditional clause (roughly 'if/when' or 'one who'), followed by با تو 'with you'. جنگ آورم is a verb phrase where جنگ (fight/war) is the object and آورم ('I bring/cause') expresses 'I wage/bring about war' — first person singular present/optative.
segments.s416-g128: به here marks manner/means ('by/with'). پرخاش means 'an assault' or 'verbal/physical attack'. خوی پلنگ is a genitive construction 'the disposition/nature of a leopard' (i.e. leopard‑like ferocity). آورم again is 'I bring/unleash' — the speaker promises to bring leopard‑like assaults.
segments.s416-g81: همه (subject) = 'everyone/all'; از من is a prepositional phrase 'from me / because of me' placed after the subject for emphasis; انگار (here) means 'it seems/they suppose' and is followed by the vocative ای پهلوان ('O champion'), so the whole segment reads 'everyone, as if because of me, (they say), O champion'.
segments.s416-g82: بدی (noun) 'harm/evil' is the semantic subject of the small clause; ناید is an archaic/persian poetic form meaning 'does not come' (3rd-person), so 'بدی ناید' = 'harm will not come'. از شاه خود = 'from his/their own king' (خود = 'own'); بیگمان is an adverb meaning 'without doubt / certainly' (archaic).
segments.s416-g83: ازان پس که = 'after that, when' (temporal connective). من is the first-person subject; تاج بر سر نهم is an older poetic order where نهم (from نهادن) = 'I place/put' and بر سر = 'on (my) head', so 'I put the crown on my head.'
segments.s416-g84: جهان را is the definite object marked by را ('the world'); به دست تو = 'into your hand' (to you); اندر نهم repeats the verb نهم ('I put/establish') in older poetic position after the prepositional phrase, so the clause means 'and I place the world into your hand.' Note the object precedes the verb as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s416-g85: نه ... باشد ... گناه is a negative clause: نه (negation) + predicate + noun. نزدیک دادار = 'near the Creator' (دادار = epithet for God); باشد is the classical third-person form of 'to be' used here to mean 'there will be / it is the case that'; گناه (at the end) is the noun 'sin' serving as the predicate: 'It will not be sin before the Creator.'
segments.s416-g86: نه ... آیدم ... نیز ... از روی شاه: another negative clause continuing the thought. شرم 'shame' is the noun; آیدم (آید + م) = 'comes to me' (1st person), so 'shame will not come to me'; نیز 'also/too' links to the previous clause; از روی شاه = 'from the face/presence of the king' i.e. 'before the king.' So the line means 'nor will I be ashamed before the king.'
segments.s416-g87: چو = 'when' (temporal); تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'; بازگردی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive or classical form of 'to return' ('you return'); به زابلستان = 'to Zabulistan' (destination). The clause is a temporal subordinate: 'When you return to Zabulistan...'.
segments.s416-g88: به هنگام = 'at the time of / when'; بشکوفه is a poetic/stative form meaning 'in blossom / when it blossoms'; گلستان = 'garden/rose-garden' (the place that blossoms). Together: 'at the time the garden is in bloom.'
segments.s416-g137: This is an instance of reported speech introduced by the prepositional phrase + verb + subject: بدو گفت رستم = “Rostam said to him.” The conjunction که introduces the reported clause. کنم is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative (کردن → کنم), used here for deliberation or a self-question (“shall I do…”); the subject “I” is omitted and understood from the verb ending. ایدون is a modifier of the clause (temporal/now/interrogative in sense) and belongs with کنم to form the deliberative question.
segments.s416-g138: This clause continues the deliberative question: شوم here is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of شدن (‘become’/auxiliary) used idiomatically with کنم to mean “shall I…?”; جامه راه is a compound meaning “traveling garb/robe.” بیرون کنم places the action at the end (typical Persian verb-final ordering) with کنم again in the subjunctive, so the whole phrase reads “shall I put on travel-clothes and go out (on the road).”
segments.s416-g139: به یک هفته is a temporal phrase meaning “for/through one week.” نخچیر کردم is a past action with کردم (I did/hunted); همی is an archaic narrative/continuous particle marking repeated or ongoing action (roughly “continually” or progressive past). Word order places the time phrase first, then the verb phrase.
segments.s416-g140: به جای introduces an expression of substitution “in place of / instead of.” The noun sequence جای بره (جای = “instead of”, بره = “a lamb”) is followed by the object of خوردن; خوردم is the simple past “I ate.” همی again gives a continuous/iterative coloring to the statement.
segments.s416-g141: به هنگام خوردن is a temporal adverbial “at the time of eating / at mealtime.” مرا باز خوان is an imperative clause: خوان here functions as a verb meaning “call/summon,” with باز adding the sense “back/again” and مرا the object “me.” The object marker را is separated in the segmentization (م + را), but the whole phrase is a command “call me back.”
segments.s416-g142: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” بنشینی is the 2nd/3rd‑person subjunctive/present (you may sit / when you sit) used in temporal subordinate clauses. با دوده is an instrument/condition (‘with soot/smoke on you’), and از پیش خوان locates the sitting “before the table/at the feast.” The clause is therefore a condition/time clause describing when to call.
segments.s416-g143: ازان جایگه is a locative/origin phrase “from that place.” رخش را is the definite object (را marks the horse Rakhsh), and برنشست is a compound past verb meaning “he sat upon / mounted (it).” Word order puts the source phrase first, then object, then the past verb, a typical narrative ordering.
segments.s416-g144: دل‌خسته را marks the patient/theme (‘the weary heart’) with را; اندر اندیشه is a locative/mental phrase “into thought/inwardly,” and بست is the past of بستن (‘to bind, to close’). Together this is an idiomatic expression meaning he withdrew into thought or ‘wrapped/closed his weary heart in thought.’
segments.s416-g49: خنک is an adjective/adverb placed before the verb phrase and describes how the time should pass ('pleasantly' or 'coolly'). زال + کش is a compound in which کش is the productive agentive element attached to the name زال, yielding 'the one who kills/slays Zal'; بگذرد is the 3rd person singular subjunctive/present (optative) 'may pass'. روزگار is the noun governed by the verb ('time, days'). Word order here is adjective/adverb + agentive noun + verb + subject noun phrase, yielding an optative clause.
segments.s416-g50: به گیتی bماند is another optative: به + گیتی 'in/on the world' and بماند is 3rd person singular subjunctive 'may (it) remain'. ترا را together form the archaic/object pronoun 'to you/for you' (here written as clitic parts 'ت' + 'را') and is the beneficiary/possessive reference for یادگار 'a memento'; the clause literally says 'may a memento remain in the world for you.'
segments.s416-g51: بدیدم is the simple past 'I saw'; ترا (here written as ت + را) is the enclitic object pronoun 'you' (تو را), so 'بدیدم ترا' = 'I saw you'. یادم آمد is an idiom 'it came to my memory' or 'I remembered', where یاد + م (the 1st‑person clitic forming یادَم 'my memory') + آمد 'came' puts the remembered item after the verb: زریر follows as the thing/person remembered ('Zarir came to my mind / I remembered Zarir').
segments.s416-g52: This segment is a string of epithets applied to the named person: سپهدار is a title 'commander', اسپ‌افگن is a descriptive compound 'horse‑breaker' or 'tamer/one who unhorses', و is the coordinating 'and', نره شیر is a compound meaning 'male lion' (نره modifying شیر). The sequence lists heroic attributes in apposition to زریر.
segments.s416-g53: بدو is the prepositional/locative clitic 'to him/unto him' attached before the verb; گفت here functions as the past 'said' (the lemma listing is conversational but context shows the past-tense verb 'گفت'). رستم is the subject doing the saying. که‌ای is an archaic interrogative address 'who are you' (که + ای) followed by پهلوان 'champion' — a vocative/interrogative formula.
segments.s416-g54: A string of epithets coordinated by و: جهاندار (an honorific 'lord/sovereign of the world'), و بیدار ('and awake/alert'), و روشن‌روان ('and bright‑souled' or 'clear‑minded'). Each adjective follows the vocative and together they form the address's descriptive complements; there is no copula because Persian often lists predicates in this paratactic way.
segments.s416-g55: یکی 'one' quantifies آرزو 'a wish'; آرزو دارم is the idiomatic present 'I have a wish' (دارم = 'I have'). از شهریار marks the source/recipient 'from the king' (i.e. 'a wish of mine addressed to the king' or 'a request from the king'). Word order is subject-like quantifier + noun + verb + prepositional phrase indicating to/from whom.
segments.s416-g56: که introduces the content/purpose clause 'that...'. باشم is 1st person singular subjunctive 'that I may be' (here expressing hope/desired state). بران is an archaic prepositional/adverbial particle 'on that / with respect to that', linking to آرزو 'that wish', and کامگار is the predicate 'successful/satisfied' describing the desired outcome — 'that I may be successful with respect to that wish.'
segments.s416-g17: نشینیم is a 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive form meaning “let us sit.” یکجای (postposed to the verb) specifies location/adverbially “in one place/together.” و simply coordinates the two clauses. پاسخ دهیم is a parallel 1pl hortative phrase “let us give an answer,” with پاسخ as the object and دهیم (give) in the same mood as نشینیم.
segments.s416-g18: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here (roughly “indeed/now”), placed before the prepositional phrase. در سخن is a locative/prepositional phrase “in/within speech/words.” رای فرخ is a noun phrase “a fortunate/favorable plan/counsel” (رای = plan/counsel; فرخ = fortunate) with the modifier after the noun, and نهیم is the 1pl subjunctive/hortative ‘let us place/set (it)’ linking back to the previous hortative clause.
segments.s416-g19: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative phrase “know/consider it thus.” که introduces the subordinate clause. In یزدان گوای منست the word order places یزدان (God) with گوای (witness) as a predicative noun and منست functioning as a copular/predicative element — i.e. “God is witness (for me).” The clause is an assertive explanatory remark introduced by that/که.
segments.s416-g20: This is a verbless/predicative clause meaning “wisdom is my guide in this speech.” خرد is the subject “wisdom.” زین سخن is a classical expression “in/from this speech/these words” (زین = ‘از + این’ in older usage) that modifies the predicate; رهنمای is the predicate noun “guide,” and منست again functions as the copular/predicative element “is (to me).”
segments.s416-g21: که introduces an explanatory clause “for/as.” من is the subject “I.” زین سخنها is “from these words” (classical ز + این + plural marking) and functions as a source phrase; نجویم is the negative present/subjunctive first-person form “I do not seek,” and فروغ is the object “radiance/glory.” Word order places the negated verb after subject and source phrase, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s416-g22: نگردم is the negative present/subjunctive or potential form “I will not turn/be turned.” به هر کار is a prepositional phrase “to/for any affair/any matter” (هر = every/any, کار = affair/matter). گرد دروغ here expresses movement or inclination “toward falsehood” (گرد used classically to indicate turning/around), so the whole means “I will not be turned toward falsehood in any matter.”
segments.s416-g23: که here functions as a conditional introducer (“for/if”), making this clause counterfactual. روی سیاوش‌گر is a noun phrase “a face like Siyâvash’s” (سیاوش‌گر = ‘Siyâvash-like,’ the -گر suffix indicating resemblance). دیدمی is an older past form “I had seen” or “I saw” (1st person), so the clause reads “if I had seen a face like Siyâvash’s.”
segments.s416-g24: بدین is the classical compound meaning “to/unto this” (ب = to/into + دین = this in older usage). تازه‌رویی is a noun meaning “the fresh-faced/newcomer” (literally “fresh-faced one”). نگردیدمی is the negative past/subjunctive form meaning “I would not have turned” or “I did not turn,” here completing the counterfactual: “I would not have turned to this fresh-faced one.”
segments.s416-g145: بیامد is a past (perfective) verb ‘came’ used narratively. دمان here is a locative noun meaning an edge/foothill (poetic 'verge'); تا introduces an 'until' clause that is completed by رسید. The clause order is somewhat inverted/poetic: verb (بیامد) appears before the locative phrase and the subordinate 'تا ... رسید' (‘until he reached the iwan’).
segments.s416-g146: رخ is the (direct) object 'face/appearance' and is followed by a sequence of proper names in apposition (زال، سام، نریمان) naming whose faces were seen. بدید is the archaic/poetic past form of دیدن ‘to see’ (here 'saw'); object precedes the verb as typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s416-g147: بدو here is the prepositional form 'to him' (بد + او = 'to him'), so بدو گفت functions as 'he said to him.' کای is a vocative 'O' and مهتر نامدار is the addressed phrase 'renowned/illustrious chief' (مهتر = chief/elder, نامدار = famed).
segments.s416-g148: رسیدم is first-person past 'I arrived / I came'; here it reports the speaker's arrival. به نزدیک + proper name expresses proximity 'near Esfandiyar' (به = to/toward, نزدیک = near). Word order places the verb before the locative phrase in this reported speech.
segments.s416-g149: سواریش = سوار + ـش 'his rider/horseman' (possessive suffix). دیدم is first-person past 'I saw.' چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and سرو سهی is a poetic simile 'a tall/dark cypress' (سرو = cypress; سهی describing color/poise). The object precedes the verb, and the simile phrase follows immediately.
segments.s416-g150: This is a string of adjectival/participial descriptions: خردمند 'wise' followed by coordinated phrases introduced by با ('with' / 'possessing')—با زیب 'with beauty' and با فرهی 'with nobility/cultured dignity.' The repeated با is emphatic and marks qualities possessed by the rider.
segments.s416-g151: تو گفتی که is the reporting frame 'you said that' with که introducing a subordinate clause. شاه فریدون is the subject 'King Fereydun.' گرد here is a poetic prepositional/relational particle meaning roughly 'concerning / in the circle of / in (his) greatness' and links to the following noun phrase.
segments.s416-g152: بزرگی دانایی is a compound noun phrase 'greatness (بزرگی) of wisdom / great wisdom' that functions as the thing involved in the entrusting. او را is the marked direct object 'him' (را is the definite/object marker) placed before the verb, and سپرد is the past transitive 'entrusted / gave over.' Word order places the thing entrusted before the object pronoun and then the verb.
segments.s416-g1: بفرمود is a narrative/honorific past verb meaning 'he ordered/said' and introduces an imperative clause. کاسپ سیه is a noun (name or noun) followed by the adjective سیه (Persian places adjectives after the noun). زین کنید is a polite/plural imperative (کنید) meaning 'saddle/put a saddle on' with زین as the direct object.
segments.s416-g2: به بالای او is a prepositional phrase marking the goal/place ('onto his back'); بالای is 'upon/above' and او is the pronoun 'him'. زین زرین کنید repeats the imperative with the noun زین 'saddle' followed by the adjective زرین 'golden' (adjective after noun), and کنید is the polite/plural imperative 'do/put'.
segments.s416-g3: پس از is the compound preposition 'after' (پس + از) introducing the originating group. لشکر نامور is a noun + adjective ('the renowned army/host'); صدسوار is a compound numeral noun 'a hundred horsemen' that functions as the group being described.
segments.s416-g4: برفتند is past, 3rd-person plural 'they went/rode off' (verb follows the noun phrase in this poetic order). با is the preposition 'with' and فرخ here is an epithet 'fortunate' attached to the proper name اسفندیار; together فرخ اسفندیار names the person accompanying them.
segments.s416-g5: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came/reached'. دمان ('hem/skirt' or 'edge') is the noun subject that follows the verb (poetic inversion). تا here means 'as far as' marking extent, and لب هیرمند is a locative phrase 'the bank/brink of the Helmand (river)'.
segments.s416-g6: به فتراک is 'to/onto the saddle-cloth' (به + noun). بر گرد کرده is a verbal phrase where گرد 'around' combines with the perfect participle کرده 'having made/done' to mean 'having made/placed around', and کمند is the object 'lasso/snare' — together 'had looped a lasso around the saddle-cloth.'
segments.s416-g7: ازین سو is a frozen locative expression 'from this side' composed of از + این (here spelled ین) + سو ('side'). خروشی is a noun 'a roar/cry' and برآورد here is used as a past verb 'he raised/emitted' (poetic verb choice), with رخش (the steed's name) as the subject placed after the verb.
segments.s416-g8: وزان is a condensed conjunctive locution ('and from that/thereupon'). روی اسپ is a locative combination where روی functions as 'upon/on' + اسپ 'horse/steed'. یل is an epithet 'hero/brave man' modifying the proper name تاج‌بخش; the whole phrase identifies the opposing horseman/steed encountered at that moment.
segments.s416-g65: نشاید is an impersonal/archaic predicate meaning “it is not fitting” or “one should not.” It governs the following verbal phrase گذر کردن (verbal noun گذر + light verb کردن) meaning “to pass over / to disregard.” از introduces the object رای تو (“your counsel/opinion”), so the whole segment says “it is not fitting to pass over your counsel.”
segments.s416-g66: گذشت is used here as a verbal idea of leaving/passing (past or verbal-noun sense) with an omitted subject (poetic ellipsis). از بر و بوم = “from (your) realm/land”; وز is a poetic contraction of و + ز (and from) introducing جای تو (“your place/dwelling”). In other words, “(one) would pass away from your land and from your dwelling.”
segments.s416-g67: ولیکن is a formal/poetic adversative particle meaning “but.” ز is the poetic preposition “from,” and فرمان شاه جهان is a genitival phrase “the command of the king of the world” (king = sovereign). The whole segment sets up the source from which the speaker will not deviate.
segments.s416-g68: نپیچم is negative + verb in the first-person singular (ن + پیچ + ending -م), here meaning “I will not turn aside / I do not swerve.” آشکار و نهان (“openly and secretly”) gives the scope: the speaker will not deviate either publicly or in private. روان is a poetic adverbial/qualifying word here (an intensifier meaning something like “at all times”/“in motion”); its exact nuance is literary rather than strictly lexical.
segments.s416-g69: به زابل is a prepositional phrase “to/at Zabul.” نفرمود is the negative past third-person of فرمودن (“he did not say/command”). ما را is the object “us” with the object marker را. درنگ is a verbal noun/infinitival idea meaning “delay / tarry,” functioning as the thing he did not command.
segments.s416-g70: نه introduces a second negative element (“nor”). با نامداران این بوم is a prepositional phrase “with the renowned/illustrious men of this land,” and جنگ is a verbal noun meaning “war / to make war,” completing the idea: he did not tell them to tarry, nor to wage war against the famous men of this realm. The verb is omitted again by poetic ellipsis but understood from the previous clause.
segments.s416-g71: تو (you) is the subject; آن کن is an imperative phrase “do that” (آن = “that,” کن = imperative “do”). که introduces a subordinate/result clause. بر یابی is a present/subjunctive-like form meaning “you may attain / you will gain” (بر- as a prefixed direction/intensifier + یابی), and از روزگار specifies the source/benefit “from fate / from the times.” The structure is imperative + purpose/result clause.
segments.s416-g72: بران رو is an imperative sequence: بران (thence/thereon) + رو (imperative of رفتن “go”) meaning “go thence / depart (there).” که introduces the reason/timing clause in which فرمان دهد شهریار (“the king will give a command”) uses دهد (present/subjunctive third person) to express the forthcoming action. The whole second segment tells the addressee to depart because the sovereign will issue orders.
segments.s416-g97: بترسم is a first-person present/subjunctive/optative form (prefix ب‑ + root ترس) meaning 'I fear' or 'I am afraid that…'. که is the subordinate conjunction 'that'. چشم بد آید همی is a small clause with poetic word order: 'چشم بد' = 'evil eye/evil glance' and آید (present) + همی (epic continuous particle) gives the sense 'may come/strike continually'.
segments.s416-g98: This clause continues the fear: سر از خواب خوش 'the head from pleasant sleep' is inverted (subject after prepositional phrase). برگراید is an archaic/poetic verbal form (prefixed/derived from گردیدن/برگشتن) in the present/subjunctive sense 'may be turned / may be roused'; همی again gives a continuous/epic nuance.
segments.s416-g99: همی یابد is a habitual/epic present ('he/one finds, will find'); یابد is the present of یافتن used here as 'find/encounter'. اندر میان is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in the midst of', and دیو راه is a genitive construction 'the way/path of a demon'.
segments.s416-g100: دلت is دل + second-person enclitic ت meaning 'your heart'. کژ کند is a verbal phrase: کژ (adjective 'crooked/awry') with کند 'makes/does' gives 'will make crooked / will turn aside' (future/optative sense in context). از پی means 'from/in pursuit of', followed by the coordinated objects تاج و گاه 'crown and (high) place/rank'.
segments.s416-g101: This is an impersonal evaluative clause: یکی ننگ باشد 'it would be a disgrace/shame'. باشد is the classical present/subjunctive form used to express 'would be'. مرا (here split as م + را in the text) is the dative/object 'to me' (the dataset lists м and را separately). زین سخن = 'from/for this speech/these words' (زین = 'from this/this').
segments.s416-g102: که introduces the content clause. تا here functions as a purpose/temporal connector ('so that / until') and جاودان is an adjective 'eternal'. آن refers back to the thing just mentioned; نگردد is negative subjunctive/passive of گشتن/گردیدن 'not become / not turn into', and کهن is the predicate adjective 'old/ancient'.
segments.s416-g103: که here resumes/explains the previous clause ('for / since / when'). چون functions temporally ('when' / 'since') rather than merely 'like'. تو is the second-person pronoun. سپهبد is the office/title 'spahbed (general)'; گزیده is a past participle/adjective 'chosen/selected', and سری is an epithet 'head/chief' (appositional).
segments.s416-g104: This segment lists epithets modifying the addressed commander: سرافراز 'highly honored/proud', شیری an adjective 'lion-like / leonine' used metaphorically, و coordinates the next epithet, and گندآوری is a deverbal/adjectival compound 'bringer of corruption/ruin' used poetically as an epithet.
segments.s416-g129: فرامش کنم is a first‑person present/subjunctive/jussive construction literally 'I do forget' but here idiomatically 'I would forget' or 'I should forget'; مهر نان و نمک is the direct object (مهر = kindness/favour, نان و نمک = 'bread and salt', i.e. hospitality), placed after the verb as is common in poetic diction.
segments.s416-g130: به من indicates direction 'to/for me' and بر here functions as a directional/prepositional element 'toward' (classical word order separates the particles); دگرگونه 'different/otherwise' modifies the verb گرد(د) 'become/turn' (گردد is subjunctive/optative 'should become'); فلک is the subject 'the sky/heavens' used metonymically for fate.
segments.s416-g131: وگر is the conditional 'if'; سربپیچم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of سرپیچیدن 'to turn one's head aside / to evade', here 'if I turn aside/evade'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; فرمان شاه is a genitive sequence 'the king's command'.
segments.s416-g132: بدان is the imperative/prepositive verb 'know (this)' addressed to the hearer; گیتی 'the world' is the subject; آتش بود جایگاه is a verbless/predicative sequence meaning 'the place would be fire' (بود is the past/be‑copula used here under the conditional sense 'would be'), so the clause states 'know that the world would be a place of fire.'
segments.s416-g133: The pronoun 'ترا' is metrically split into ت + را (ت = 2nd‑person element, را = object marker) placed before آرزوگر 'a wisher/aspirant' (the subject) for emphasis; چنین آمدست is a frozen phrase 'has thus come' (آمدست = 'has come'). The overall order foregrounds 'to you' then names the person who has come.
segments.s416-g134: یک امروز 'for one day / at least today' (یک = one/emphatic); با here = 'together/with'; می‌بساییم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive of بساییدن (here 'to serve/ to be of use / to reconcile'), and دست 'hand' is the direct object — idiomatically 'let us join/offer hands' (make peace or cooperate for a day).
segments.s416-g135: که داند commonly functions as the idiom 'who knows'; the second که introduces the indirect question 'what' (فردا چه شاید) — 'what tomorrow may (bring/do)'; بدن 'the body' is the object of the implied verb 'shall happen to'. The clause expresses uncertainty about the future.
segments.s416-g136: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (here written as ب + دین in the manuscript split); داستانی 'in this tale/affair' (داستانی as a noun), نباید is the impersonal modal 'one must not/should not', and زدن is the infinitive 'to strike/hit' (used idiomatically for acting violently or harming). The whole clause says 'one should not act violently in this matter.'
segments.s416-g57: خرامان is an adverb of manner ('with a stately/proud gait') placed before the verb for poetic effect. بیایی is a 2nd-person present-subjunctive/optative (here a polite imperative: 'may you come' / 'come'), followed by سوی + noun giving direction; خان من is a possessive phrase 'my khan / my lord' (خان = lord, positioned before the enclitic من). Word order is adverb → verb → direction → possessor.
segments.s416-g58: به introduces the noun دیدار ('to/for a meeting/visit'). روشن is an adjective modifying دیدار ('a bright/clear meeting'); کنی is the 2nd-person singular present-subjunctive of کردن used causatively ('you make / you cause'). جان من is the affected noun phrase 'my soul' (جان = soul). The clause thus says that the act of meeting will cause the speaker's soul to be brightened.
segments.s416-g59: سزای تو is a genitive phrase 'the recompense/desert of you' (what is due to you). گر is the conditional particle 'if'. نیست is the negated copula 'is not' and is followed by the indefinite چیزی که هست ('anything that exists'). The whole clause means 'if there is nothing that is your due / if nothing exists that is fitting for you.'
segments.s416-g60: بکوشیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative ('let us strive' / 'we should try'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. با آن 'with that' (آن = that/it) introduces the instrument. بساییم is 1st-plural present-subjunctive of بساییدن meaning 'to be of use / to benefit / to serve'; دست ('hand') here names the thing to be used or the agency (literal 'hand' but idiomatically 'effort/power'). The clause expresses intent to try and make use of that thing by our hand/effort.
segments.s416-g61: چنین 'thus' modifies the noun پاسخ ('answer'). آوردش is the past tense verb آوردن with the enclitic object -ش (آورد + ش) meaning 'he brought/gave it to him' or 'he answered him'; the subject اسفندیار follows the verb (inverted order typical of classical poetry). So the line reports that Esfandiyar responded in this way.
segments.s416-g62: که‌ای here introduces the reported speech/relative content ('that you are' / 'you who are'); که functions as a complementizer/relative particle. از یلان جهان is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of/from the heroes of the world' and یادگار (noun) means 'memorial/memento' — i.e. 'a living memorial/remnant of the world's heroes.' Word order is compressed as in poetry: complementizer → predicate phrase.
segments.s416-g63: هرانکس 'whosoever' with کجا 'wherever/anywhere' forms an indefinite clause. چون تو باشد is a comparative clause 'is like you' (چون = like/as; باشد = 3rd-person subjunctive/correlative 'be/should be'). به نام here means 'in name / in reputation' (نام used as 'fame'). The clause sets up a general condition: anyone anywhere who is like you in fame.
segments.s416-g64: همه شهر ایران is a partitive/genitive phrase 'all the cities of Iran' or more broadly 'the whole realm of Iran.' بدو is a prepositional + enclitic pronoun 'to him' (بـ + دو/شو contracted), and شادکام is a predicative adjective 'fortunate/blessed.' The segment says that all Iranian cities will regard such a person as fortunate.

sentences.s416-s77: More came to visit upon hearing the news; from him the kingly glory continually shone.
sentences.s416-s17: May all your enemies be filled with fear because of you; may the hearts of the ill‑fated be split in two.
sentences.s416-s18: May your fortune be victorious every year; may the dark nights be Nowruz for you.
sentences.s416-s19: When Esfandiyar heard his speech, he came down from the famed spot.
sentences.s416-s20: He took Piltan into his arms; when he was pleased he raised a cry of praise.
sentences.s416-s53: May you never come into my house; do not be my guest in this frontier.
sentences.s416-s54: If you can pull this sharp point out of the brain, try, and with an incantation restore the weary one.
sentences.s416-s55: Whatever you desire of me I will grant; the sight of you will give peace to my soul.
sentences.s416-s56: Except for the link that had been freed from the chain, there was a rupture — there was a shameful deed.
sentences.s416-s21: Praise be to God, O world‑champion, for I saw you joyful and of bright spirit.
sentences.s416-s22: It is fitting to praise you; may the champions of the world be but your dust.
sentences.s416-s23: How pleasant that he should have a son like you; he sees one branch that is over him.
sentences.s416-s24: Blessed is he who had you as his support; he was safe from the harshness of fate.
sentences.s416-s57: Let no one see me alive in chains; my radiant soul is above it — that is enough.
sentences.s416-s58: Before you there were others who came, but they did not put my foot into heavy fetters.
sentences.s416-s59: In answer Esfandiyar thus said to him: 'O you, a remembrance among your like in the world.'
sentences.s416-s60: You have spoken entirely the truth and have not told a lie; men of splendor do not take to crookedness.
sentences.s416-s5: Thus, when they came near the water, both hastened to meet one another.
sentences.s416-s6: Tahmatan dismounted from his mount, came down to the river on foot, and gave the hero a greeting.
sentences.s416-s7: Then, having uttered praise, he said that from the one God he had always wished that there should be a guide.
sentences.s416-s8: that he, accompanied by famed men, might come to this place in such health and with an army.
sentences.s416-s37: Put a fetter on your own foot at once; there will be no shame in being bound by the emperor.
sentences.s416-s38: When they bring you bound before the king, all the blame will fall on him.
sentences.s416-s39: From this heavy obligation of mine my heart is weary; in your presence I have girded my waist.
sentences.s416-s40: I will not remain so that you should remain in bonds until night; but if any harm comes to you from anything...
sentences.s416-s13: Nothing will remain for me except Siyavakhsh — that crown-bearer, bestower of the world.
sentences.s416-s14: Lucky indeed is the king who has a son like you; the father boasts up on high and with exultation.
sentences.s416-s15: Blessed is the city of Iran that worships your throne — may your fortune remain awake.
sentences.s416-s16: Ill-fated is the man who fights you; he will seek (it), and from the throne he will come down into the fray.
sentences.s416-s45: From me you will also find many wishes, so that your land and home may be adorned.
sentences.s416-s46: Rostam said to him, 'O famed one, I have long sought (this) from the Judge, the Creator.'
sentences.s416-s47: That I may make my heart glad by seeing you; now, how could I desire to do you harm?
sentences.s416-s48: We are two proud-necked ones — old and young, wise and alert — two champions.
sentences.s416-s61: But Peshotan knows what the king commanded — that I should set out on the road.
sentences.s416-s62: If I now come to your khan, I shall be at home — happy and a victorious guest of yours.
sentences.s416-s63: You bend your neck at the king's command; for me the brightness of day turns black.
sentences.s416-s64: And if again I make war with you, I will bring upon you assaults with the nature of a leopard.
sentences.s416-s41: All, as if because of me, O champion, believe that surely no harm will come from their king.
sentences.s416-s42: After that, when I place the crown on my head and put the world into your hand.
sentences.s416-s43: There will be no sin in the sight of the Creator, nor will I feel shame before the king.
sentences.s416-s44: When you return to Zabulistan, at the time the rose-garden is in bloom.
sentences.s416-s69: Rostam said to him, “What shall I do now? Shall I don my travelling clothes and go out on the road?”
sentences.s416-s70: For a week I hunted continually; instead of a lamb I ate an onager.
sentences.s416-s71: Call me back at mealtime; when you sit before the table with soot on you.
sentences.s416-s72: From that place he mounted Rakhsh; he wrapped his weary heart in thought.
sentences.s416-s25: May the days pass pleasantly for the slayer of Zāl; may a memorial of you remain in the world.
sentences.s416-s26: I saw you; Zarir came to my mind — commander, horse‑breaker, and a male lion.
sentences.s416-s27: Rostam said to him, 'Who are you, champion, sovereign, awake and bright‑souled?'
sentences.s416-s28: I have one wish of the king: that I may be successful in that wish.
sentences.s416-s9: Let us sit together and give an answer; let us set a fortunate plan forth in our speech.
sentences.s416-s10: Know it thus: God is my witness; wisdom is my guide in these words.
sentences.s416-s11: For I do not seek radiance from these words; I will not be turned toward falsehood in any affair.
sentences.s416-s12: For had I seen the face like Siyâvash’s, I would not have turned to this fresh-faced one.
sentences.s416-s73: He came up to the verge until he reached the iwan, and he saw the faces of Zal, Sam, and Nariman.
sentences.s416-s74: He said to him, 'O renowned chief, I have come near Esfandiyar.'
sentences.s416-s75: I saw his horseman, like a tall dark cypress, wise and possessing beauty and nobility.
sentences.s416-s76: You said that King Fereydun, in his greatness, entrusted him with wisdom.
sentences.s416-s1: He commanded, “Saddle the black Kasp; put a golden saddle upon his back.”
sentences.s416-s2: After the renowned host, a hundred horsemen rode forth with the fortunate Esfandiyār.
sentences.s416-s3: The hem reached as far as the bank of the Helmand; they had looped a lasso about the saddle-cloth.
sentences.s416-s4: From that side Rakhsh let out a cry; and at that the steed of the hero Taj‑Bakhsh faced (or charged) the horse.
sentences.s416-s33: It is not fitting to pass over your counsel, nor to leave your land and your dwelling.
sentences.s416-s34: But I will not turn aside from the command of the king of the world, neither openly nor secretly.
sentences.s416-s35: He did not command us to tarry in Zabul, nor to make war with the renowned men of this land.
sentences.s416-s36: Do that which will win you favor with fate; then go thence, for the king will give the command.
sentences.s416-s49: I fear that the evil eye may come, and that it may rouse him from his pleasant sleep.
sentences.s416-s50: He may find, in the midst of a demon's path, that your heart turns aside in pursuit of crown and rank.
sentences.s416-s51: It would be a shame to me because of these words if that did not become ancient forever.
sentences.s416-s52: For when you, the chosen spahbed, proud-headed, lion-like and a bringer of ruin...
sentences.s416-s65: I would forget the kindness of bread and salt if the heavens were to turn differently toward me.
sentences.s416-s66: But if I turn my head away from the king's command, know that the world would be a place of fire.
sentences.s416-s67: A suitor/one who desires you has come thus; let us, for today, join hands.
sentences.s416-s68: For who knows what tomorrow may do to the body? Therefore one should not strike (i.e. act violently) in this matter.
sentences.s416-s29: Come striding to my presence; by a clear meeting you will brighten my soul.
sentences.s416-s30: If there is nothing that is your due, let us strive, and with that let our hand be of service.
sentences.s416-s31: Esfandiyar thus gave him this answer: 'You are a memorial of the heroes of the world.'
sentences.s416-s32: Whosoever, wherever, is like you in renown, the whole cities of Iran will count him fortunate.
